author: @garybotha5133
text:
I worked in the coal industry for 35 years in SA.
Coal power stations utilize coal with approximately 2.5% Sulphur content.  If you extrapolate this into tons of Sulphur Dioxide emissions this equates to approximately 75000 tons of Sulphur dioxide from 1 coal fired power station !!! South Africa has 20 coal fired plants when they are working. 
China has over 3000 coal fired power stations.  75000 tons Sulphur dioxide x 3000 = 225 million tons of Sulphur Dioxide  emissions per year.  They are the biggest polluters on the planet. Sulphur dioxide scrubbing is expensive and produces a lot of waste. If scrubbing are  being utilized then the waste is stored close to the plant. Google earth doesn't show this. 
Great show. Please do a segment on this.

Unfortunately there are many types of deposits, more or less already structured to make it graphite appropriate quality.

author: @youcanthandlethetruth6976
text: To everyone going on and on about Coal Production. Carbon is everywhere, we don't need to shave mountain tops to produce carbon, carbon is not bad for the environment. If you are talking about the 'Gases' that come off of carbon which is encapsulated within it's porous structure and that which is contained therein, is what is actually bad for the environment. Then all we need to do is put that gas into a system of pipes to condense that gas and turn it into Oil and Gasoline and everything else we need. If Volcanoes can actively erupt for the last 4.6 billion years? I think Man Kind will be okay to play with carbon and gaseous combustibles.

author: @JehuMcSpooran
text: Coal is far from a dying industry. It is massive here in Australia. Currently we a flogging it off to the Chinese along with our iron ore and the Adani group have been planning a massive mine in North Queensland to power most of India. Coal is vital to the iron industry for the smelting process and also for the supply of clinker for the cement industry. Still, a nice idea though.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I did a bit of due dilligence mate - apparently the rumour is that indian mine isn't going to happen - the indian secretary for energy wants production to be all in india and is opening indian coal mines at a rate of 4 a month - i understand there are funding issues too and concerns over the port. I believe chinese exports have fallen dramatically too and steel production has more or less collapsed - any probably a matter of opinion for a while - i guess time will tell

replies.author: @JehuMcSpooran
replies.text: The mining boom has died down a lot here in regards to Iron ore but it is still massive. There are huge coal mines all the way up the coast. The last flight I was on from Brisbane to Sydney you could see all the coal mines in the North Sydney to Hunter Valley region. Looked like a golf course with black sand traps. Driving the highways next to them makes you feel like a flea. As for the Adani mine, there has been a lot of opposition to it here due to the damage it will cause but knowing our government, it will go through.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: no worries mate - like i say - time will tell

author: @DeliciousDeBlair
text:
Depending on where you live, you have one thing you need to check before simply diving into a pile of coal or tailings though...

https://pubs.usgs.gov/fs/1997/fs163-97/FS-163-97.html

and 

https://www.scientificamerican.com/article/coal-ash-is-more-radioactive-than-nuclear-waste/

Other than that, I have certainly shared the idea with my team since we are building up a wood gasification plant which will produce organic char/coke type materials as a byproduct, and as such, while exploring a host of different feedstocks in pure, and mixed formats, we are hoping to explore re roasting and post roasting carbon contents and structures both  for their conductivity and their porosity.

I have an idea that cracked fullerenes would make superbly porous material although perhaps the conductivity might be in question. 

Just how conductive would you deem as an 'ideal' level of conductivity for theoretical purposes?

Seeing as the nuclear industry wants nothing less than https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radiophobia the last thing they would do is produce publications leading the general public to think about it.

If you believe that they ['the nuclear industry'] would for any reason increase the awareness or induce any additional  fear and/or stress toward their already dangerous and increasingly unpopular product, clearly you understand neither human psychology nor the market economy.

Neither the coal nor the nuclear industry were involved in producing any reports which would decrease their public acceptance.

replies.author: @shogunx
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith  I have no problem with disposing of waste in a positive way, but you have to play the chess game all the way out.  Say that turns into an industrial scale process.  Now you have significant investment into infrastructure so you can process the waste.  Great.  Since there is no more active production of the waste itself, sooner or later either a) your recycle plant lies fallow or b) people start mining coal again to fulfill your industrial needs.  See the problem?  Same deal as the US Military-Industrial complex, who must keep making  War to stay in business.

author: @juanj2116
text: So, here's a thought. Graphite is 20K a ton due to its scarcity. If you make an easy inexpensive method to convert coal into graphite, then graphite will no longer be worth 20k a ton. In turn, if graphite is a key component of Lithium-ion batteries, then this process would be bringing down the cost of Lithium-ion batteries (due to lower cost of graphite). Since Lithium-ion batteries is the biggest competitor to your graphene/graphite/hemp batteries.....isn't this process that you are researching helping your competitors???

author: @NeoShameMan
text:
Good job!

I want to apologized for the other video and the comment on the solar graphene, haha! It didn't come out as I intended lol. I was exciting by the idea of turning typical carbon into graphene instead of sourcing graphite! And this video is another step in that direction! There is things I want to pursue once I finished whatever job I'm doing now, and since your series of video, I'm always surveying the "carbon chain", recently some pre teen kids turn Styrofoam into activated carbon despite all adults telling them that was impossible! Now they are giving talk and filling patents lol It's like we could have a chain from *waste* to opportunity all the way down! And myself I'm interested to see if we can create home grown autonomous factory using basically only raw material, and right now the concern is can we have solar and transistor with carbon/graphene. Hence my reaction! it was me thinking at loud on your amazing achievement! Just keep going, that's a fantastic job you are doing, I don't have a th of your resources or knowledge, but knowing it's possible is enough for me to follow the trail and experiment when I will be ready!

author: @geodeaholicm4889
text: very cool, you continue to amaze.  i think charicterizing coal will be more complex then you might expect.  in addition to the range between pure hi grade coal & pure spoil in any given mine there will also be major differences in grade of coal (anthracite to bituminous  to sub-bituminous to lignite etc) from area to area.  also the minerology of the spoil will vary.

author: @ImplyDods
text: Great idea Robert. Let's hope the oil companies don't see this and buy up all the slack.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: to be honest mate if it brought jobs to areas that needed it and didn't spoil the environment i would be upset if they did - but really it doesn't impinge on oil really

author: @justanotheryoutuber739
text: very interesting video. maybe you could invest in a better microphone? it is probably a bad mixture of your camera microphone and my phone speakers, but I think a microphone would really improve the videos!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: my microphone is a zoom h2n mate - i don't think there is a better one

author: @JoClip1984
text: I absolutely love your idea! I hope that this gives you some excellent exposure

replies.author: @JoClip1984
replies.text:
I mean, honestly, I'm astounded that you've hit upon and shared the bare bones of such a potentially game changing process!
This is wonderful news and I am, not for the first time, excited to see what your future brings the world.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: thank you mate

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
